annotations.label: Prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: the prisoner's dilemma is a thought experiment involving two rational agents

annotations.note: Mathlib has no game-theory library and no definition of the prisoner's dilemma.

annotations.label: Poundstone's premise

anchor.snippet: Two members of a criminal gang are arrested and imprisoned

annotations.note: Narrative example, not formal mathematical content present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three possible outcomes for A and B

anchor.snippet: This leads to three different possible outcomes for prisoners A and B

annotations.note: No formalization of game outcomes for prisoner's dilemma exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Defection is best response

anchor.snippet: Regardless of what the other decides, each prisoner gets a higher reward by betraying the other

annotations.note: No notion of best response or dominant strategy exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mutual defection is the unique strong Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: Defection always results in a better payoff than cooperation, so it is a strictly dominant strategy for both players

annotations.note: Nash equilibrium and dominant strategies are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized payoff structure

anchor.snippet: If both players cooperate, they both receive the reward

annotations.note: No payoff matrix or normal-form game definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prisoner's dilemma in the strong sense

anchor.snippet: to be a prisoner's dilemma game in the strong sense, the following condition must hold for the payoffs

annotations.note: The strong-form payoff inequalities are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interpretation of payoff inequalities

anchor.snippet: implies that mutual cooperation is superior to mutual defection

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain any results about ordering of cooperative vs. defective payoffs.

annotations.label: Iterated prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: If two players play the prisoner's dilemma more than once in succession, remember their opponent's previous actions, and are allowed to change their strategy accordingly, the game is called the iterated prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: No definition of repeated/iterated games appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extra iterated condition

anchor.snippet: the iterative version also requires that

annotations.note: No iterated-PD payoff inequalities are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite iteration: defect all rounds

anchor.snippet: If the iterated prisoner's dilemma is played a finite number of times and both players know this, then the dominant strategy and Nash equilibrium is to defect in all rounds

annotations.note: Backward-induction equilibrium theorems are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aumann's cooperation theorem

anchor.snippet: As shown by Robert Aumann in a 1959 paper

annotations.note: Aumann's folk-theorem-style result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Experimental strategies (AER 2019)

anchor.snippet: According to a 2019 experimental study in the American Economic Review

annotations.note: Empirical study, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Axelrod's tournament

anchor.section: Axelrod's tournament and successful strategy conditions
anchor.snippet: Interest in the iterated prisoner's dilemma was kindled by Robert Axelrod

annotations.note: Axelrod's tournament is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tit for tat

anchor.section: Axelrod's tournament and successful strategy conditions
anchor.snippet: The strategy is simply to cooperate on the first iteration of the game; after that, the player does what his or her opponent did on the previous move

annotations.note: The tit-for-tat strategy is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tit for tat with forgiveness

anchor.section: Axelrod's tournament and successful strategy conditions
anchor.snippet: a slightly better strategy can be "tit for tat with forgiveness"

annotations.note: No game-theoretic strategies are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axelrod's conditions for successful strategies

anchor.section: Axelrod's tournament and successful strategy conditions
anchor.snippet: Axelrod stated several conditions necessary for a strategy to succeed

annotations.note: Axelrod's four conditions are informal heuristics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Win-stay, lose-switch (Pavlov)

anchor.snippet: In the strategy called win-stay, lose-switch , faced with a failure to cooperate, the player switches strategy the next turn

annotations.note: The Pavlov strategy is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Southampton colluding strategy

anchor.snippet: a team from Southampton University in England introduced a more successful strategy

annotations.note: Empirical tournament strategy, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Memory-n strategy

anchor.snippet: If P is a function of only their most recent n encounters, it is called a "memory-n" strategy

annotations.note: Mathlib has no memory-bounded strategy formalism.

annotations.label: Memory-n reduces to memory-1

anchor.snippet: It has been shown that for any memory-n strategy there is a corresponding memory-1 strategy that gives the same statistical results

annotations.note: Press–Dyson-style reductions of memory-n to memory-1 strategies are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transition matrix M

anchor.snippet: a transition matrix M may be defined for X whose ij -th entry is the probability

annotations.note: Mathlib defines stochastic matrices in general, but no transition matrix specifically for an iterated game is formalized.

annotations.label: Stationary vector existence

anchor.snippet: One result of stochastic theory is that there exists a stationary vector v for the matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has stochastic-matrix infrastructure but no Perron/stationary-distribution existence theorem for stochastic matrices in this form.

annotations.label: Zero-determinant (ZD) strategies

anchor.snippet: William H. Press and Freeman Dyson published a new class of strategies for the stochastic iterated prisoner's dilemma called "zero-determinant" (ZD) strategies

annotations.note: ZD strategies of Press–Dyson are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extortionary ZD strategies

anchor.snippet: the ZD space also contains strategies that, in the case of two players, can allow one player to unilaterally set the other player's score

annotations.note: Extortion strategy results are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unfair ZD strategies are not evolutionarily stable

anchor.snippet: It has been shown that unfair ZD strategies are not evolutionarily stable

annotations.note: Evolutionarily stable strategy theory is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ZD extortion loses in large populations

anchor.snippet: Theory and simulations confirm that beyond a critical population size, ZD extortion loses out in evolutionary competition

annotations.note: Evolutionary game-theory results are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generous ZD strategies

anchor.snippet: another ZD class called "generous" strategies is both stable and robust

annotations.note: Generous ZD strategies are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Akin's good strategies

anchor.snippet: so-called "good" strategies, which were defined by Ethan Akin

annotations.note: Akin's class of good strategies is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous iterated prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: some researchers have looked at models of the continuous iterated prisoner's dilemma, in which players are able to make a variable contribution to the other player

annotations.note: Continuous-action games are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cooperation harder in continuous case

anchor.snippet: Le and Boyd

annotations.note: Le–Boyd's analytical result on continuous IPD is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Climate change

anchor.snippet: In environmental studies , the dilemma is evident in crises such as global climate change

annotations.note: Applied example outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Guppies and vampire bats

anchor.snippet: Cooperative behavior of many animals can be understood as an example of the iterated prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: Biological example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Addiction as intertemporal PD

anchor.snippet: George Ainslie points out that addiction can be cast as an intertemporal prisoner's dilemma problem between the present and future selves of the addict

annotations.note: Psychology example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Cigarette advertising

anchor.snippet: Advertising is sometimes cited as a real example of the prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: Economics example, not formal mathematics in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cartels

anchor.snippet: Without enforceable agreements, members of a cartel are also involved in a (multi-player) prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: Economics example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Doping in sport

anchor.snippet: Doping in sport has been cited as an example of a prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: Applied example outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Security dilemma

anchor.snippet: A classic example is the security dilemma

annotations.note: International-relations example, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tragedy of the commons

anchor.snippet: Garrett Hardin 's tragedy of the commons may be viewed as an example of a multi-player generalization of the prisoner's dilemma

annotations.note: Multi-player game example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newspaper boxes in New Zealand

anchor.snippet: describes a situation in New Zealand where newspaper boxes are left unlocked

annotations.note: Anecdotal example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Chambliss exam boycott

anchor.snippet: the classroom experiment conducted by sociology professor Dan Chambliss at Hamilton College in the 1980s

annotations.note: Anecdotal example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Johns Hopkins boycott

anchor.snippet: a similar incident occurred at Johns Hopkins University in 2013

annotations.note: Anecdotal example, no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Closed-bag exchange

anchor.snippet: Two people meet and exchange closed bags, with the understanding that one of them contains money, and the other contains a purchase

annotations.note: Thought-experiment variant, no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Friend or Foe?

anchor.snippet: Friend or Foe? is a game show that aired from 2002 to 2003

annotations.note: Game-show example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: The Traitors finale

anchor.snippet: The dilemma is also a Game Mechanic on The Traitors if multiple Traitors remain in the game as finalists

annotations.note: Game-show example, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Iterated snowdrift game

anchor.snippet: the "Iterated Snowdrift Game" may more closely reflect real-world social situations

annotations.note: Snowdrift game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coordination games

anchor.snippet: In coordination games, players must coordinate their strategies for a good outcome

annotations.note: Coordination games are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymmetric prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: A more general set of games is asymmetric

annotations.note: Asymmetric PD is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impure prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: A prisoner's dilemma is considered "impure" if a mixed strategy may give better expected payoffs than a pure strategy

annotations.note: Mixed-strategy refinements of PD are not in Mathlib.
